petitioner_background: The petitioner is a musician from Nepal seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. He is a member of two bands, a hardcore metal band and a punk rock band, and has recorded music at a studio in Massachusetts. The petitioner's bands have performed in various venues and have had some success in Nepal, including a nomination for a Best Song in a Foreign Language award.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a January 3, 2012 article discussing the petitioner's formation of a hardcore metal band, his music, performance, and history. An online printout showed the circulation of the publication and its relation to other publications in Nepal was consistent with a major media publication.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director found that the petitioner met this criterion and the AAO agreed. The evidence in the record supported this finding because the article was about the petitioner and his work, and the publication's circulation and standing in Nepal was consistent with a major media publication, satisfying the requirements of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner, a musician, submitted evidence of performances as a performing artist, asserting his work had been displayed at artistic exhibitions or showcases.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director found the petitioner met this criterion, but the AAO reversed on de novo review. The AAO held that the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vii) limits this criterion to evidence relating to the visual arts, an interpretation upheld in Negro-Plumpe v. Okin, which held that performances by a performing artist do not fall under this regulation. Since the petitioner is not a visual artist and has not created tangible pieces of art on display at artistic exhibitions or showcases, he did not meet this criterion. The AAO cited its authority to deny a petition on grounds not identified by the director per Spencer Enterprises and Soltane.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner asserted his role in two bands (a hardcore metal band and a punk rock band) established he met this criterion. Evidence included: (1) a nomination for a Best Song in a Foreign Language category award; (2) an April 2, 2014 letter from a copy editor and former band member calling the award 'prestigious'; (3) an April 6, 2014 letter from a journalist comparing the award to 'an American Music Award or Grammy Award'; (4) an online printout describing the radio station's award process and nationwide listener poll; (5) an online source calling the awards 'one of the most coveted awards in the Nepali music industry'; (6) a 2005 editorial article from a Nepali publication stating the band was one of 'two bands [that] have been integral in the underground movement'; (7) a cover story chronicling bands in Nepal; (8) another article about the band's formation and music; (9) reference letters from a blogger, a music promoter, a music label owner, a studio owner who recorded the band's album, and the lead singer of another Nepali rock band; (10) a 2012 article characterizing the band as 'a little known band that still sings in Nepali'; (11) a 2014 interview in a weekly publication targeting urban readers in Nepal with circulation of 30,000; (12) a 2014 interview where the petitioner said 'We do not have fans, all we have [are] our family and friends'; (13) materials from various blogs including one where the blogger stated his blog had 12 readers; (14) a page from a book stating 'In North America, music blogs are replacing print music-journalism.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that while the petitioner showed he performed a leading or critical role for the two bands, he did not show that either band had a distinguished reputation. An ability to secure performance opportunities and produce favorably reviewed albums is indicative of a band's viability, not a distinguished reputation. Regarding the first band: the band was only nominated (not awarded) the Best Song in a Foreign Language award, and the only publication listing nominees in that category had no viewership information. Reference letter claims that the award was prestigious were conclusory without supporting details on candidate pool size, selection criteria, media attention, or industry recognition. The radio station poll lacked evidence of national reach, and online sources lacked verification of reliability. The editorial article about Nepal's music scene generally did not show the magazine's reach or influence. The other article did not characterize the band in a manner consistent with distinguished reputation, and the magazine's claimed circulation of 42,000 was disputed by media sources. Reference letters were conclusory without supporting evidence per Matter of Soffici and Matter of Treasure Craft. Regarding the second band: reference letters were from those associated with the petitioner's bands. Published materials characterized the band as 'a little known band,' and the petitioner himself admitted having no fans beyond family and friends. The 30,000 circulation weekly was insufficient, and no circulation/impact information was provided for another publication. Blog materials do not carry the same weight as major media because they lack editorial/verification processes, and the petitioner showed no evidence of the blogs' significance (one blogger admitted having 12 readers). Materials published after the April 18, 2014 filing date could not establish eligibility per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1), (12), Matter of Katigbak, Matter of Wing's Tea House, Matter of Izummi, and Matter of Bardouille. The petitioner's own self-assessment as 'a little known dude from an unknown hardcore band' was not indicative of distinguished reputation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner did not submit any evidence relating to his earnings or earning information of others in the same field.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded the petitioner did not meet this criterion. On appeal, the petitioner did not assert he meets this criterion and therefore abandoned the issue per Sepulveda v. United States Att'y Gen. and Hristov v. Roark. Moreover, the petitioner submitted no evidence of his earnings or comparable earnings of others in the field.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to satisfy the antecedent regulatory requirement of submitting qualifying evidence of a one-time achievement or evidence meeting at least three of the ten regulatory criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). The petitioner only met one criterion (published material). Although the AAO noted it did not need to provide a final merits determination under Kazarian, it added that a review of the evidence in the aggregate, including evidence referenced on appeal, supported a finding that the petitioner had not demonstrated through extensive evidence the level of expertise required for the extraordinary ability classification. The petitioner did not demonstrate sustained national or international acclaim or that he is one of the small percentage at the very top of his field.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011), aff'd, 683 F.3d 1030 (9th Cir. 2012)
Spencer Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 229 F. Supp. 2d 1025 (E.D. Cal. 2001), aff'd, 345 F.3d 683 (9th Cir. 2003)
Soltane v. Dep't of Justice, 381 F.3d 143 (3d Cir. 2004)
Negro-Plumpe v. Okin, 2:07-CV-820-ECR-RJJ, 2008 WL 10697512 (D. Nev. Sept. 8, 2008)
Matter of Soffici, 22 I&N Dec. 158 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg'l Comm'r 1972)
Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45 (Reg'l Comm'r 1971)
Matter of Wing's Tea House, 16 I&N Dec. 158 (Reg'l Comm'r 1977)
Matter of Izummi, 22 I&N Dec. 169 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998)
Sepulveda v. United States Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO reversed the director's favorable finding on the exhibition criterion under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vii) without prior notice, invoking its authority under Spencer Enterprises and Soltane to deny on grounds not identified by the director on de novo review. The petitioner also submitted materials published or posted after the April 18, 2014 filing date, which the AAO rejected as ineligible to establish eligibility per the rule that a petitioner must demonstrate eligibility at the time of filing. The petitioner abandoned the high salary criterion by failing to raise it on appeal.
